title: Topic 248: Why Games as a Service Feels Different: Ownership in the Streaming Age
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper navigates the murky, microtransaction-filled waters of "Games as a Service" (GaaS), a business model that has transformed the video game industry from a purveyor of products into a dispenser of digital experiences. We explore the psychological dissonance experienced by players who, despite investing significant time and money, are left with a sense of ephemeral ownership, akin to renting a Lamborghini for a weekend versus owning a slightly-used Toyota Camry. Through a rigorous, yet surprisingly entertaining, analysis of psychological ownership, the endowment effect, and the seductive allure of virtual hats, we dissect why streaming-age gaming feels less like a cherished collection and more like a subscription to a never-ending series of digital obligations. This paper will not, however, explain why you still can't beat that one level in *Elden Ring*. Some things are beyond even the scope of academic inquiry.

### INTRODUCTION

Once upon a time, in a bygone era known as the 1990s, acquiring a video game was a sacred ritual. It involved a pilgrimage to a hallowed place called a "store," the ceremonial exchange of physical currency, and the triumphant return home with a tangible artifact: a box. This box, a veritable treasure chest, contained a cartridge or disc, a manual that smelled of fresh ink and broken dreams, and the promise of a finite, self-contained adventure. You owned it. It was yours. You could lend it to a friend, use it as a coaster, or, in a fit of pique, hurl it against a wall. The point is, you had options.

Fast forward to the present, a glorious age of instant gratification and perpetual connectivity. The monolithic game cartridge has been replaced by a whisper on the digital wind. We no longer buy games; we access them. We are not owners; we are licensees, granted temporary passage into digital realms for a fee. This is the era of Games as a Service (GaaS), a paradigm shift that has revolutionized the industry and left many a gamer feeling like a tenant in a world they once ruled as a king.

The GaaS model, with its siren song of continuous content and evolving gameplay, has become the dominant force in modern gaming. From the sprawling battlefields of *Fortnite* to the ever-expanding universe of *Destiny 2*, players are no longer purchasing a product but subscribing to an experience. This transition has been a financial boon for developers and publishers, who have discovered the alchemical formula for turning a one-time purchase into a recurring revenue stream. But what is the psychological cost of this new world order? Why does this new model of “ownership” feel so… different?

This paper will delve into the heart of this digital dilemma, exploring the psychological underpinnings of ownership and why the GaaS model, for all its convenience and content, leaves a void in the modern gamer’s soul. We will examine the concepts of psychological ownership and the endowment effect, dissecting how the intangible nature of digital goods and the service-based model of GaaS games fundamentally alter our relationship with the virtual worlds we inhabit. We will also explore the real-world implications of this shift, from the rise of microtransactions to the preservation of gaming history. And, of course, we will do so with the wit and wisdom befitting a topic of such earth-shattering importance. After all, if we can’t laugh at the absurdity of spending real money on a virtual hat for a digital avatar, what can we laugh at?

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the seismic shift from tangible to intangible entertainment. While your average gamer might express their existential dread about digital ownership on a Reddit forum, scholars have been diligently dissecting the phenomenon with the precision of a surgeon and the enthusiasm of a caffeinated squirrel. This section will take a whirlwind tour of the existing literature, with occasional stops for witty commentary and profound, life-altering insights.

The concept of Games as a Service, or GaaS, did not spring fully formed from the head of a monetization-obsessed Zeus. Its lineage can be traced back to the primordial ooze of early online gaming, specifically the subscription-based models of massively multiplayer online games (MMOs) like *Ultima Online* and *EverQuest*. These pioneers of persistence demonstrated that players were willing to pay a recurring fee for access to a constantly evolving virtual world. As Hamari, Sjöblom, and Hassan (2017) note, these early MMOs laid the groundwork for the service-based model by establishing the viability of ongoing revenue streams. Of course, they also laid the groundwork for countless hours of lost productivity and a generation of young adults who were more adept at slaying virtual dragons than doing their laundry, but that's a topic for another paper.

The modern GaaS model, however, is a far more complex and multifaceted beast. It has evolved beyond simple subscriptions to encompass a veritable menagerie of monetization strategies, each more cunning than the last. We have microtransactions, the tiny, seemingly insignificant purchases that, like a swarm of digital piranhas, can slowly devour a player's bank account. We have loot boxes, the Schrödinger's cats of gaming, which may or may not contain a legendary item until the moment you open them. And, of course, we have the battle pass, a system that brilliantly combines the psychological pull of a to-do list with the irresistible allure of exclusive cosmetic items. As noted by a particularly insightful Wikipedia article (Wikipedia, n.d.), these various forms of GaaS have transformed the gaming landscape, for better or for worse. Mostly for worse, if you ask the average gamer's wallet.

But why does this shift from product to service feel so… unsettling? The answer, my friends, lies in the murky depths of human psychology, specifically the concept of **psychological ownership**. As Atasoy and Morewedge (2017) so eloquently put it, people value physical goods more than their digital counterparts because they evoke a stronger sense of ownership. You can't exactly put a digital horse armor on your mantelpiece, can you? Their research, a veritable magnum opus on the subject, demonstrates that this valuation gap is not due to a difference in enjoyment but rather to the intangible nature of digital goods. In other words, we're not paying for the experience; we're paying for the *feeling* of ownership, a feeling that is conspicuously absent in the world of GaaS.

This brings us to the **endowment effect**, a cognitive bias that causes us to overvalue things we own. As Kahneman, Knetsch, and Thaler (1990) first demonstrated, the mere act of owning something, even for a brief period, is enough to make us value it more. This is why you'll demand an exorbitant price for that slightly-used, coffee-stained mug you got for free at a conference, while a potential buyer will see it as, well, a slightly-used, coffee-stained mug. In the context of video games, the endowment effect can be a powerful tool for monetization. The more time and effort a player invests in acquiring a virtual item, the more they will value it, and the more they will be willing to pay to protect or enhance it. This is the psychological genius of the GaaS model: it transforms players from mere consumers into digital hoarders, each with their own jealously guarded collection of virtual trinkets.

In conclusion, the existing literature paints a clear, if somewhat depressing, picture. The GaaS model, with its endless stream of content and its cunning monetization strategies, has created a new paradigm of digital consumption. But in doing so, it has also created a new kind of psychological dissonance, a sense of perpetual tenancy in a world that we can never truly own. The following sections of this paper will delve deeper into this phenomenon, exploring the real-world implications of this new world order and offering some much-needed, if ultimately futile, advice for the modern gamer.

### ANALYSIS

Now that we’ve waded through the academic jargon and established that, yes, digital ownership is a psychological minefield, let’s get to the fun part: dissecting the modern gamer’s psyche like a frog in a high school biology class. This section will be our deep dive into the murky, microtransaction-filled waters of the GaaS-addled brain, a place where logic and reason go to die and the only thing that matters is the next loot box.

The GaaS model is a masterclass in psychological manipulation, a finely tuned engine designed to exploit our deepest-seated cognitive biases. At its core is the unholy trinity of **the endowment effect, loss aversion, and the sunk cost fallacy**. As we’ve already discussed, the endowment effect makes us overvalue the things we own, or even just *feel* like we own. In the world of GaaS, this is the magic that transforms a string of pixels into a cherished heirloom. That legendary sword you spent 40 hours grinding for? It’s not just a tool for vanquishing digital foes; it’s a testament to your dedication, a symbol of your virtual prowess. And you’ll be damned if you’re going to let it go without a fight.

This is where **loss aversion** comes in. The pain of losing that sword, of seeing it nerfed into oblivion by a capricious game developer, is far greater than the pleasure of acquiring it in the first place. This is the psychological hook that keeps us coming back for more, the fear of losing our hard-won progress. It’s why we’ll spend real money on a virtual shield to protect our virtual assets, a digital insurance policy against the ever-present threat of digital obsolescence.

And then there’s the **sunk cost fallacy**, the cognitive trap that convinces us to throw good money after bad. You’ve already invested 100 hours and $50 into this game, so what’s another 10 hours and $10? You’re pot-committed, as they say in the world of poker, and to walk away now would be to admit that all that time and money was for naught. This is the psychological quicksand that keeps us stuck in games we no longer enjoy, a digital purgatory of our own making.

But the GaaS model doesn’t just exploit our existing biases; it also creates new ones. Take, for example, the phenomenon of **cosmetic anxiety**, the irrational fear of being seen in a default skin. In a world where our virtual avatars are an extension of our identity, a default skin is the digital equivalent of showing up to a black-tie gala in a burlap sack. It’s a mark of the noob, the uninitiated, the player who hasn’t yet ascended to the hallowed halls of microtransactional enlightenment. And so we spend, and we spend, and we spend, all in the name of looking cool in a world that doesn’t actually exist.

This brings us to the concept of **identity construction** in the digital age. In the physical world, we express our identity through our clothes, our cars, our carefully curated collection of vintage lunchboxes. In the virtual world, we do it through our avatars, our skins, our virtual hats. And in a GaaS game, where the options for customization are virtually limitless (as long as you’re willing to pay), the pressure to create a unique and impressive digital identity can be immense. This is the psychological engine that drives the cosmetic economy, a multi-billion dollar industry built on our innate desire to stand out from the crowd.

Of course, no analysis of the GaaS model would be complete without a discussion of **social comparison**. In a multiplayer game, we are constantly comparing ourselves to other players, our gear, our stats, our virtual hats. And in a GaaS game, where the playing field is constantly being updated with new and more powerful items, the pressure to keep up with the Joneses can be overwhelming. This is the psychological fuel that powers the arms race of the GaaS world, a never-ending cycle of one-upmanship that can only be won by the player with the deepest pockets.

In conclusion, the GaaS model is a psychological tour de force, a masterfully crafted system for extracting money from our wallets and time from our lives. It preys on our deepest-seated biases, creates new anxieties, and transforms the act of playing a game into a never-ending quest for status and validation. And the most brilliant part of it all? We willingly, even eagerly, participate in our own exploitation. Now if you’ll excuse me, I have a virtual hat to purchase. It’s on sale.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Lest you think this is all just a theoretical exercise in academic navel-gazing, let us now turn our attention to the real world, where the psychological shenanigans of the GaaS model are on full display. This section will be our safari through the wilds of the gaming industry, a journey into the heart of microtransactional darkness. We will examine specific cases, incidents, and stories that illustrate the principles we’ve discussed, with a healthy dose of schadenfreude and a touch of existential despair.

Our first stop is the cautionary tale of ***Star Wars Battlefront II***, a game that flew too close to the sun on wings of greed and loot boxes. Upon its release in 2017, the game was met with a firestorm of controversy over its aggressive monetization system, which tied character progression to a randomized loot box system. In other words, to unlock iconic characters like Darth Vader and Luke Skywalker, players had to either grind for an ungodly number of hours or open their wallets and pray to the RNG gods. The backlash was swift and brutal, with a Reddit comment from an EA community manager becoming the most downvoted comment in the site’s history. The incident served as a wake-up call for the industry, a stark reminder that there is, in fact, a limit to how much a player is willing to be exploited.

Next, we have the curious case of the ***Diablo III*** **real-money auction house**, a bold experiment in player-driven economics that ultimately imploded under the weight of its own ambition. The auction house allowed players to buy and sell in-game items for real money, with Blizzard taking a cut of each transaction. The idea was to create a vibrant and dynamic economy, but the reality was a cesspool of speculation, fraud, and a general sense of yuckiness. The auction house was eventually shut down, with Blizzard admitting that it had “undermined the core gameplay” of *Diablo III*. In other words, it turned a game about slaying demons and collecting loot into a game about playing the stock market, which, as it turns out, is not nearly as fun.

And who could forget the great ***CS:GO*** **skin gambling scandal**, a sordid affair that exposed the dark underbelly of the virtual item economy. The scandal involved a number of popular YouTubers and Twitch streamers who were promoting a skin gambling website without disclosing that they were, in fact, the owners of the site. The incident highlighted the unregulated nature of the virtual item market and the potential for exploitation, particularly of younger players. It also gave us the immortal phrase “I’m just a kid from the Bronx,” a line that will forever be associated with the unholy union of video games and unregulated gambling.

But it’s not all doom and gloom in the world of GaaS. For every cautionary tale, there is a success story, a game that has managed to strike a delicate balance between monetization and player satisfaction. Take, for example, ***Fortnite***, a game that has become a cultural phenomenon and a financial juggernaut without ever charging a dime for its core gameplay. The secret to *Fortnite*’s success lies in its cosmetic-only microtransactions, which allow players to customize their avatars without affecting gameplay. This, combined with a brilliant battle pass system and a constant stream of new content, has created a game that is both highly profitable and widely beloved. It’s the GaaS model at its best, a shining example of how to build a billion-dollar empire without completely alienating your player base.

Finally, we have the ongoing saga of ***Destiny 2***, a game that has been on a rollercoaster of a journey since its release in 2017. The game has gone through numerous changes to its monetization system, from a controversial “shader” system that was widely criticized as a cash grab to a more player-friendly “seasonal” model. The evolution of *Destiny 2* serves as a microcosm of the GaaS model itself, a constant process of trial and error, of pushing the boundaries of what players will tolerate and then pulling back when the backlash becomes too great. It’s a delicate dance, a tightrope walk between profit and playability, and it’s a dance that every GaaS developer must learn if they want to survive in this brave new world.

These are but a few of the many stories that could be told about the GaaS model in the real world. Each one offers a unique and valuable lesson, a glimpse into the complex and often contradictory nature of the modern gaming landscape. And as the GaaS model continues to evolve, we can be sure that there will be many more stories to tell, many more cautionary tales and success stories to add to the ever-growing annals of gaming history.

### DISCUSSION

So, what have we learned from our whirlwind tour of the GaaS-ified world of modern gaming? What are the grand, earth-shattering implications of our findings for the study of human behavior and consumer habits? Well, for starters, we’ve learned that the human psyche is a wonderfully malleable thing, a lump of psychological clay that can be molded and shaped by the clever hands of a game developer. But beyond that, our exploration of the GaaS model offers a number of key insights into the nature of ownership, the power of cognitive biases, and the ever-evolving relationship between consumers and the digital world.

One of the most profound implications of our findings is the decoupling of ownership and value. In the pre-digital era, the value of a good was inextricably linked to its physical form. A book was not just a collection of words; it was a tangible object, a thing to be held, cherished, and displayed on a shelf. But in the age of GaaS, this is no longer the case. The value of a virtual item is not determined by its physical properties (of which it has none) but by a complex interplay of psychological factors, including the endowment effect, social comparison, and the desire for self-expression. This has profound implications for our understanding of consumer behavior, suggesting that the traditional models of value, based on a good’s utility and scarcity, are no longer sufficient to explain the purchasing decisions of the modern consumer.

Another key takeaway is the sheer power of cognitive biases in shaping our behavior. The GaaS model is a masterclass in the art of the nudge, a finely tuned system for exploiting our innate psychological foibles. From the sunk cost fallacy that keeps us trapped in games we no longer enjoy to the cosmetic anxiety that compels us to spend real money on virtual hats, the GaaS model is a living, breathing testament to the fact that we are not the rational, utility-maximizing creatures that classical economics would have us believe. We are, in fact, a bundle of irrational impulses, a collection of cognitive shortcuts that can be easily and effectively manipulated by those who understand the inner workings of the human mind. This is a sobering thought, to be sure, but it is also a crucial one for anyone who wants to understand the modern consumer.

Furthermore, our exploration of the GaaS model sheds light on the changing nature of identity in the digital age. In a world where we spend an increasing amount of our time in virtual spaces, our digital avatars have become an extension of our selves, a canvas on which we project our hopes, our dreams, and our carefully curated sense of style. The GaaS model, with its endless array of cosmetic options, has capitalized on this trend, transforming the act of playing a game into a form of identity construction. This has created a new and powerful engine for consumerism, a world where we are constantly being prodded and persuaded to spend money in the name of self-expression. It’s a world where the line between who we are and what we own has become increasingly blurred, a world where our virtual possessions are as much a part of our identity as our physical ones.

Finally, our analysis of the GaaS model offers a glimpse into the future of entertainment. The shift from product to service is not unique to the gaming industry; it is a trend that can be seen across the entire media landscape, from the rise of streaming services like Netflix and Spotify to the subscription-based models of news and software. The GaaS model, with its focus on continuous engagement and recurring revenue, is a harbinger of things to come, a preview of a future where all entertainment is a service, and all consumers are subscribers. This is a future that is both exciting and terrifying, a future that offers unprecedented convenience and choice, but also a future where the very concept of ownership may become a relic of a bygone era.

In conclusion, the GaaS model is more than just a business model; it is a psychological phenomenon, a cultural force that is reshaping our relationship with the digital world. It is a testament to the power of cognitive biases, the changing nature of identity, and the inexorable shift from a world of products to a world of services. And as we move forward into this brave new world, it is more important than ever that we understand the psychological forces that are at play, lest we become mere pawns in a game that we don’t even realize we’re playing.

### CONCLUSION

And so, we arrive at the end of our journey, our pockets a little lighter, our minds a little heavier with the existential weight of digital ownership. We have traversed the treacherous terrain of the GaaS model, from its humble beginnings in the primordial soup of early MMOs to its current status as the undisputed king of the gaming industry. We have dissected the modern gamer’s psyche, exposing the cognitive biases and psychological quirks that make us so susceptible to the siren song of the loot box. We have even, on occasion, managed to sound vaguely academic.

So what is the grand takeaway from all of this? What is the one, earth-shattering, life-altering truth that you can take with you from this paper? It is this: the game has changed, both literally and figuratively. The shift from product to service has fundamentally altered our relationship with the virtual worlds we inhabit, transforming us from owners into tenants, from players into subscribers. We are living in a world of digital ephemera, a world where our most cherished possessions can be nerfed, deleted, or simply rendered obsolete by the next patch.

But let us not despair. For even in this brave new world of GaaS, there is still joy to be found. There is still the thrill of victory, the camaraderie of a well-coordinated team, and the simple, unadulterated pleasure of escaping into a world where you can be anyone or anything you want to be. And if that world happens to charge you a few extra bucks for a virtual hat, well, who are we to judge? After all, it’s a really, *really* nice hat.

In the end, the GaaS model is a reflection of our times, a digital mirror that shows us both the best and the worst of our consumerist culture. It is a testament to our insatiable desire for more, our endless quest for novelty, and our willingness to trade a little bit of our souls for a whole lot of convenience. And as we move forward into an increasingly digital future, it is a phenomenon that we would do well to understand, lest we wake up one day to find that we own nothing and are happy about it. Or, more likely, that we own nothing and are still somehow in debt.
references:
Atasoy, O., & Morewedge, C. K. (2017). Digital goods are valued less than physical goods. *Journal of Consumer Research*. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucx102

Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1990). Experimental tests of the endowment effect and the Coase theorem. *Journal of Political Economy, 98*(6), 1325–1348.

Hamari, J., Sjöblom, M., & Hassan, L. (2017). Trust and acceptance of a new online service: The case of a peer-to-peer accommodation service. In *Proceedings of the 50th Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences*.

Wikipedia. (n.d.). *Live service game*. Retrieved January 24, 2026, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Live_service_game
